answer:
**Iqbal's "Wataniyat" (Nationalism), a poem in *Bang-e-Dara Part 3*, portrays territorial nationalism as a modern "idol" that supplants Islamic unity and faith.**[^1] He depicts the Nation as the "greatest" of "newer gods," wearing "Faith’s own final desecration," sowing rivalry among peoples, promoting conquest over trade, politicizing land over divine command, and severing "the roots of Islam’s brotherhood."[^2] The poem urges Muslims to reject confinement to "one place" as "calamity," embracing a prophetic, oceanic freedom instead.[^3]

**Charles Comte's *Traité de Législation Vol. II* treats patriotism (alongside love and friendship) as a core, benevolent "moral affection" essential to human perfection.**[^4] In Chapter 1, he frames it as part of properly directed passions that foster social harmony when aligned with "enlightened intelligence" and humanity's interests—contrasting with malevolent ones like hatred or cruelty.[^5] Even among "barbarous" American tribes (Ch. 17), Comte observes patriotism as a universal trait, though subdued by antisocial passions in uncivilized states; civilized nations elevate it via moral/intellectual progress.[^6] Nations/peoples emerge as aggregates of perfected individuals shaped by climate, labor, and security, with patriotism aiding population growth and security.[^7]

**A productive disagreement could stage between Iqbal's Chapter 25 (*Wataniyat*) and Comte Vol. II Chapters 1 & 17:** Iqbal indicts nationalism as inherently divisive idolatry eroding universal brotherhood; Comte defends patriotic affection as natural, improvable virtue binding individuals into prosperous nations. Iqbal prioritizes supra-national faith ("Your homeland is Islam"); Comte grounds identity in empirical moral development and local circumstances (e.g., climate's role in virtues). This clash—spiritual universalism vs. naturalistic patriotism—highlights tensions between religious cosmopolitanism and Enlightenment progressivism, without direct overlap in the corpus to resolve it.[^8]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 25 (*Wataniyat*), [translation](https://falsafa.corpus/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/25-wataniyat/translation/).

[^2]: Ibid., [p-3b9de1](https://falsafa.corpus/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/25-wataniyat/translation/#p-3b9de1), [p-97937b](https://falsafa.corpus/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/25-wataniyat/translation/#p-97937b).

[^3]: Ibid., [p-a723b2](https://falsafa.corpus/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/25-wataniyat/translation/#p-a723b2).

[^4]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL II*, ch. 1, [translation](https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-ii-20331d/01-de-ce-qui-constitue-le-perfectionnement-et-la-degr/translation/).

[^5]: Ibid., [p-c5ad71](https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-ii-20331d/01-de-ce-qui-constitue-le-perfectionnement-et-la-degr/translation/#p-c5ad71), [p-6f2f98](https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-ii-20331d/01-de-ce-qui-constitue-le-perfectionnement-et-la-degr/translation/#p-6f2f98).

[^6]: Ibid., ch. 17, [p-6d3b7e](https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-ii-20331d/17-des-vices-et-des-maladies-qui-resultent-chez-les-p/translation/#p-6d3b7e).

[^7]: Ibid., ch. 1, [p-66a950](https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-ii-20331d/01-de-ce-qui-constitue-le-perfectionnement-et-la-degr/translation/#p-66a950), [p-dfbae5](https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-ii-20331d/01-de-ce-qui-constitue-le-perfectionnement-et-la-degr/translation/#p-dfbae5).

[^8]: No direct cross-references exist; nearest TF-IDF matches to Iqbal's poem are other Iqbal works on similar themes (e.g., *Tarana-e-Milli*, ch. 23).
